issue_summary: Change `InetAddress`-`String` conversion methods to preserve the scope ID.
javadoc_before:
/** * Returns the {@link InetAddress} having the given string representation. * *

This deliberately avoids all nameservice lookups (e.g. no DNS). * *

Anything after a {@code %} in an IPv6 address is ignored (assumed to be a Scope ID). * *

This method accepts non-ASCII digits, for example {@code "１９２.１６８.０.１"} (those are fullwidth * characters). That is consistent with {@link InetAddress}, but not with various RFCs. If you * want to accept ASCII digits only, you can use something like {@code * CharMatcher.ascii().matchesAllOf(ipString)}. * * @param ipString {@code String} containing an IPv4 or IPv6 string literal, e.g. {@code * "192.168.0.1"} or {@code "2001:db8::1"} * @return {@link InetAddress} representing the argument * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the argument is not a valid IP string literal */
javadoc_after:
/** * Returns the {@link InetAddress} having the given string representation. * *

This deliberately avoids all nameservice lookups (e.g. no DNS). * *

This method accepts non-ASCII digits, for example {@code "１９２.１６８.０.１"} (those are fullwidth * characters). That is consistent with {@link InetAddress}, but not with various RFCs. If you * want to accept ASCII digits only, you can use something like {@code * CharMatcher.ascii().matchesAllOf(ipString)}. * *

The scope ID is validated against the interfaces on the machine, which requires permissions * under Android. * *

Android users on API >= 29: Prefer {@code InetAddresses.parseNumericAddress}. * * @param ipString {@code String} containing an IPv4 or IPv6 string literal, e.g. {@code * "192.168.0.1"} or {@code "2001:db8::1"} or with a scope ID, e.g. {@code "2001:db8::1%eth0"} * @return {@link InetAddress} representing the argument * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the argument is not a valid IP string literal */
